Tafseer:
Condemnation of those who maintain separate standards for receiving and giving:
This is not merely a declarative sentence, but contains an undercurrent of curse and condemnation. 'Tatfeef' means giving short measure or weight—showing that those who cheat in measure and weight face ruin, divine wrath, and condemnation.

This describes the characteristics of those who give short measure, showing that the discussion here is not merely about cheating in measure and weight, but about a specific type of character—namely, a person who is highly alert and sensitive when receiving measure or weight from others, never wishing to receive even a fraction less than his due; but when measuring or weighing for others, he tries to cheat them. This character bears witness that human beings are not devoid of the concept of justice and the awareness of its obligation. He knows well that there should not be different standards for receiving and giving, but they must be identical. Furthermore, he knows well that what he dislikes for himself should not be preferred for others. If he does so, it means he acts contrary to his nature, overwhelmed by selfishness, which is a clear injustice and a highly contemptible act.
Why people commit injustice despite their natural love for justice: This shows that the Creator of nature has established a criterion within human nature to distinguish between justice and injustice, and has endowed him with love for justice and hatred for injustice. Despite this, if he commits injustice, it is not because he is unable to distinguish between them or is unaware of its unjust nature, but simply because—as noted—he fails to maintain his soul's balance, overwhelmed by some desire or emotion.
A thief who breaks into others' houses never wishes for someone to break into his own; a murderer who kills others does not like anyone to threaten his life or the life of his close relatives; a person who commits adultery with others' women does not approve of anyone violating his own women's honor. Rather, if the impartial opinion of these very thieves, murderers, and adulterers could be obtained, they would confess that there should be no place in society for thieves, murderers, adulterers, and other such criminals. Society should only accommodate those who protect others' lives, wealth, and honor just as they wish their own to be protected.
Arguing for the Day of Recompense from human nature: This conduct and aspect of human nature serve as obvious testimony that he neither regards the righteous and the wicked as equal, nor approves of treating them in the same manner. Rather, his impartial decision is that they must be treated differently. This requires that Allah Almighty bring a Day to treat the righteous and the wicked in accordance with their deeds. If such a Day were not to come, it would mean that to the Creator of this world, the righteous and the wicked are equal, which is contrary to the nature He endowed within man. Here, man's own nature is used to argue for the necessity of the Day of Recompense and to establish the proof against those deniers who could not deny this testimony of their nature, yet persisted in denying the warning of the Resurrection.
A weak context of revelation: Under this verse, commentators have narrated a report stating that the Helpers (Ansar) suffered from the vice of cheating in measure and weight, and therefore this verse was revealed. However, first, this Surah is Meccan, not Madinese; second, even if the Helpers suffered from this vice, they would have suffered from it only to the extent the Meccans suffered from it—or rather, the Meccans were more likely to suffer from it because they were merchants, whereas the Helpers' primary occupation was agriculture. Third, and most importantly, this verse is not revealed primarily to condemn cheating in measure and weight, but—as noted—to state that human beings are capable of distinguishing between justice and injustice. A person commits evil contrary to the testimony of his nature, solely in pursuit of some desire. Human nature requires that a Day must come to distinguish perfectly between the righteous and the wicked. If someone denies its arrival, it is solely because he wants to avoid facing the recompense, even though this is a demand of his own nature.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 2 and 3 was combined in the original text.]

Rebuking those who close their ears to the testimony of their nature: This expresses amazement and rebukes those who are untroubled: do they not think they will be resurrected for a tremendous Day, when mankind will stand before the Lord of the worlds? That is: the fear of such a Day should exist in everyone's heart, as its testimony is present within everyone's nature. If anyone's heart is devoid of this fear, he is closing his ears to the voice of his own nature, even though that Day will not be an ordinary day, but an extremely tremendous one. On that Day, people will stand before the Lord of the worlds to be questioned about what they did, and then receive reward or punishment according to their deeds.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 4 and 5 was combined in the original text.]
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 4, 5, and 6 was combined in the original text.]

Refuting the addressees' assumption:
'Kalla' (No!) is used here to refute their vain assumption with rebuke, which was pointed to in the preceding verse. That is: they are completely untroubled, assuming they face no account or recompense in which Allah will distinguish between the righteous and the wicked; rather, they assume they will enjoy even greater ease there through their family status and deities than what they enjoy here. Addressing them, He stated: 'No! Do not ruin your Hereafter through such foolish thoughts. On that Day, there will be a vast distance between the righteous (Abrar) and the wicked (Fujjar).' The records of the wicked will be in 'Sijjeen', and the righteous—whose mention is coming—will have their records in 'Illiyyun'.

The analysis of 'Sijjeen':
The word 'Sijjeen' in the preceding verse is used as a proper noun, not in its literal meaning; therefore, the Qur'an itself has clarified it. Several such names have appeared in the Qur'an, and He has clarified them in their respective places. Some examples appeared in Surah Ad-Dahr, and 'Illiyyun' and 'Tasneem' appear further on in this Surah. In such words, primary importance belongs to their proper meaning, not their literal meaning.
'And what can make you know what is Sijjeen?' (wa ma adraka ma sijjeen) - This style is used to express the terror of 'Sijjeen', asking: 'What do you assume Sijjeen is! Do not regard it as a minor thing!' Ruined is the one whose name or deeds are recorded in it!
'A register written' (kitabum-marqoom) - It is a written register—meaning the entire record of the criminals is preserved in writing. 'In writing' points to the reality that no oversight can occur, nor is there any room to doubt its authority.
Thus, 'Sijjeen' is the name of the register in which the records of the criminals' deeds are preserved in writing, based on which the decision will be made on the Day of Resurrection as to who deserves to be cast into which level of Hell. The root of 'Sijjeen' is 'sijn', which means prison or imprisonment. In this connection, the record office of those deserving punishment is named 'Sijjeen'.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 8 and 9 was combined in the original text.]

That is: do not assume you will always remain free as you are today; rather, when that Day of Recompense occurs, destruction will be for those who denied it. On that Day, they will see that not a single word or deed of theirs has been left unrecorded, nor is there any way to escape its consequence. The criminals will cry out upon seeing it:
(What is this book that leaves not a small thing or a great thing but that it has enumerated it?) (Al-Kahf, 18:49).
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 10 and 11 was combined in the original text.]

Pointing to those who are foremost in denying the Recompense:
This identifies those who are foremost in denying the Day of Recompense: only those who are transgressors and sinners deny it. The meaning is: no one who possesses even a minor trace of justice and mercy can dare to deny it. Its testimony is present within human nature; therefore, it needs no external proof. Everyone can see it in the mirror of their own heart. However, those whose hearts have been covered in transgression and sin cannot see it.
We discussed the nature of 'udwan' (transgression) and 'ithm' (sin) in their relevant places: 'udwan' means encroaching upon others' rights, and 'ithm' means withholding what is due from oneself. Those who develop the habit of encroaching upon others' rights or withholding what is due try to find a way to deny the Recompense, so that their conscience does not correct them for their transgressions.
We noted that a person does not deny a reality because he lacks proof, but because accepting it would affect his desires and habits. When he does not want to accept something, he tries to fabricate excuses, no matter how weak. In Surah Al-Qiyamah, He states:
(Rather, man, against himself, will be a witness, even if he presents his excuses) (Al-Qiyamah, 75:14-15).

This points to their method of denial: they deny an obvious truth contrary to their own conscience. Lacking any proof, instead of answering arguments with arguments, they merely show stubbornness and defiance. It is stated: when Our verses are recited to them, they say: 'Go away, we have heard it; what is in it? These are merely stories of the ancients!'
'Ayat' refers to the proofs and arguments of the Resurrection and recompense communicated to them by the Qur'an—including those in the previous Surahs and those coming further on. They also include references to the history of those past nations who were destroyed for denying the warning. Hearing these, they dismissed them with a single phrase: 'These are stories of the ancients', implying they are unworthy of attention.

The actual cause of denial:
This reveals the actual cause: their claim that these are stories of the ancients is incorrect. These are solid realities supported by arguments in nature, within themselves, and by intellect; but the rust of their deeds has covered their hearts so completely that no light of truth can enter them.
'What they used to earn' (ma kanu yaksiboon) refers to the deeds discussed under transgression (udwan) and sin (ithm), regarding which the Qur'an bears witness that those who commit them seek excuses to deny the Recompense.
'But the rust has covered their hearts' (bal rana 'ala quloobihim) points to the divine law: the proofs endowed within nature and the capacity to reflect given to the heart benefit only those who appreciate them. If a person ignores them to follow his desires, the rust of his bad deeds begins to cover them gradually, until it covers them completely, leaving no room for any truth to enter.

That is: their desire that they will enjoy high ranks in the Hereafter if it occurs will never be realized. Having blinded their own intellect and heart, they will face the punishment of being veiled from their Lord on that Day. Being veiled means they will be deprived of His proximity, His favor, His mercy, and witnessing His light. They will not even have the opportunity to present any supplication before Him.

Then, they will enter Hell, and it will be said to them: 'This is what you used to deny.' The repetition of 'thumma' (then) shows that this will be said with emphasis to humiliate them. The meaning is: 'Look at what you opposed with such force, and taste its consequence!'
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 16 and 17 was combined in the original text.]

'Illiyyun':
'Kalla' refutes their vain assumption, just as in verse 7. That is: the righteous and the wicked will never be equal; rather, just as there is a separate register and office for the wicked, there is a separate register and office for the righteous. Their records of deeds will be in 'Illiyyun'.

Just as 'Sijjeen' was mentioned to express its terror, 'Illiyyun' is mentioned to express its grandeur and high status: 'Who can estimate its grandeur and status in this world!' It is the register of those of high status.
Since 'Illiyyun' is used as a proper noun, He clarified it: it is a written register, whose every detail is preserved, and which is supervised by Allah's close angels.

'Which those near [to Allah] witness' (yashhaduhu-l-muqarraboon) - this can mean: since this register is reserved for those near to Allah, only they will have access to it, and others will have no access. Those near to Allah are mentioned in verse 28.

The reward of the righteous:
This describes the blessings the righteous (Abrar) will enjoy. 'In pleasure' (fi na'eem) shows that they will be surrounded by blessings from all sides; wherever they look, they will see only blessings.

'On couches, looking' (ala-l-ara'iki yandhuroon) - In contrast to: 'Indeed, from their Lord, that Day, they will be partitioned' (verse 15) stated regarding the wicked, the righteous will sit on couches, looking at Allah's favors, His majesty, and His light. Further on, it is clarified that they will also look at the end of their enemies, and ask: 'Have the disbelievers been rewarded for what they used to do?' (verses 35-36).
'Nadhrah' is the radiance and joy that appears on the faces of those surrounded by blessings. It is stated: whoever looks at them will see the radiance of pleasure on their faces.

As an example of these blessings, He mentioned: they will be given to drink pure wine, sealed. This wine will be sealed, opened first for them, and its seal will be of musk.
Obviously, these attributes are intended to give a basic concept; the actual reality will be known only to those who enjoy it. However, He stated: this is what those who aspire should aspire to! This encourages the believers, and mocks those worldly individuals who spend their days and nights in pursuing the temporary and worthless pleasures of this world, while ignoring what truly deserves to be pursued.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 25 and 26 was combined in the original text.]

'Mizaj' refers to the mixture added to wine to increase its pleasure or balance its taste. It is stated: its mixture will be of Tasneem, which is a spring from which those near to Allah will drink while enjoying their assembly.

'A spring' ('ayna) is in the accusative for specification (ala-s-sabeel-il-ikhtisas), and 'biha' uses the particle 'bi' in the sense of 'in' [or 'at']. I explained this under Surah Ad-Dahr, verse 6: (A spring from which the servants of Allah drink, causing it to gush forth abundantly). Note that drinking on the banks of a spring is highly pleasing. While those infatuated with worldly wine drink it however they can—even from their hands—this is the assembly of those near to Allah; therefore, its etiquettes are noble.
Commentators have generally failed to understand the nature of 'bi' here, translating it as 'from it', which is redundant because 'its mixture is of Tasneem' made this clear. 'Bi' is used in the sense of 'in' [or 'at'] in classical Arabic, and is the only appropriate meaning here.

The reversal of roles after the results manifest:
This portrays the reversal of roles that will manifest when the results of deeds appear. First, it portrays how the wealthy mocked the poor Muslims in this world. It is stated: these criminals, whenever they found them, would make them the target of their mockery and jokes.

And whenever they passed by them, they would wink at one another.
'Marru bihim' can mean: whenever the Muslims passed by them, and also: whenever these leaders passed by the Muslims, they would wink at one another to humiliate them. Note that winking is a sharp weapon for tanz (sarcasm) and tadhleel (humiliation), and we will discuss it in Surah Al-Humazah.

That is: having behaved in this manner toward the believers, when they returned to their families, they would return jesting—boasting as if they had won a great battle. This points to their moral degradation: instead of feeling any remorse, they would boast to their families, telling how they mocked so-and-so. In Surah Al-Qiyamah, He stated: (But he denied and turned away, then went to his family, swaggering).
Recall that the believers remain fearful of God among their families, ensuring their conduct does not set a wrong precedent that leads to ruin on the Day of Judgment. In contrast, these wicked individuals boast of their bad deeds to their families, turning their descendants into wicked ones.

And they tried to ensure no one held a good opinion of the believers. Whenever they saw them, they tried to convince others: 'These are indeed astray, because they oppose our ancestral religion and regard everyone besides themselves as fuel for Hell.' Note that seeing the believers' remembrance of the Hereafter affecting others, the leaders tried to counter it by calling them deviated.

Generally, commentators have interpreted this to mean: these disbelievers were not appointed as guardians over the believers to judge them as astray and correct them. However, in my view, this is part of the disbelievers' statement—meaning: when they see the believers, they say: 'These are indeed astray; they call our ancestral religion shirk, although they were not appointed as guardians over us to criticize our ways and claim to reform us.'

After describing the disbelievers' conduct, this states the reversal of roles on the Day of Judgment. It is stated: while previously the disbelievers laughed at the believers, on this Day, the believers will laugh at the disbelievers. The believers' laughing will be justified: having established the proof upon them but they refused to reform, the disbelievers deserve no sympathy.

That is: just as they will look at the manifestations of Allah's blessings from their couches, they can also look at the state of the disbelievers in Hell whenever they wish, and even question them, as stated elsewhere.

Having shown them this, the believers will be asked: 'Have the disbelievers been rewarded for what they used to do?'
'What they used to do' (ma kanu yaf'aloon) includes the mockery they displayed in the world.
